destinations.name: Banff
destinations.summary: Alberta Rocky Mountain park with turquoise glacial lakes, record 4.5 million annual visitors, and access now controlled by shuttles, paid parking and vehicle bans.
destinations.headline: Banff 2026: World-Class Peaks Behind a Wall of Shuttles, CAD 42 Parking and 4.5 Million Visitors
destinations.body:
Banff National Park sits in the Alberta Rockies about ninety minutes west of Calgary, and Parks Canada recorded roughly 4.5 million visitors in the 2025-26 fiscal year, beating the previous high of about 4.28 million set in 2023-24. That single number explains most of what a visitor now experiences. The gate price is not the problem. A daily adult pass is CAD 12.25, seniors pay CAD 10.75, a family or group of up to seven in one vehicle pays CAD 24.50, and everyone 17 and under enters free. The annual Discovery Pass is CAD 83.50 adult, CAD 71.50 senior and CAD 167.50 family. Under the Canada Strong Pass, admission is free outright from June 19 to September 7, 2026, with 25 percent off Parks Canada camping in the same window.

Access control is where the friction lives. Moraine Lake Road has been closed to personal vehicles year-round since 2023, with narrow exceptions for accessible parking permit holders and Moraine Lake Lodge guests. The only practical way in is the Parks Canada shuttle from the Lake Louise Park and Ride at 1 Whitehorn Road, where parking is free with a reservation. Shuttle tickets are CAD 12.75 adult, CAD 6.00 senior and CAD 4.00 youth, plus a non-refundable booking fee of CAD 3.50 online or CAD 5.50 by phone. The 2026 reservation window opened April 15 at 8 a.m. MDT with 40 percent of seats, and the remaining 60 percent release at 8 a.m. MDT two days before each departure. The Moraine Lake shuttle runs June 1 to October 12, every 30 minutes from 6:30 a.m. to 5 p.m., with the last return at 7:30 p.m. and Alpine Start departures at 4 a.m. and 5 a.m. The Lake Louise Lakeshore shuttle runs May 15 to October 12, and a Lake Connector links the two lakes every 30 minutes from 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. on a first-come basis.

Driving yourself is expensive by design. The Lake Louise lakeshore lot and the Fairview Day Use Area both cost CAD 42 per vehicle per day from May 15 to October 12, 2026, and the lakeshore lot routinely fills before sunrise. A three-year paid parking pilot in the Sulphur Mountain area, which serves the gondola and the Upper Hot Springs, charges CAD 17.50 per vehicle per day from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. over the same dates. In the Banff townsite the municipality charges CAD 12 an hour downtown from May 1 to October 31 and CAD 7 an hour from November 1 to April 30, enforced 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. year-round. Free alternatives exist and are the smart move: the Train Station lot has 500 free stalls, Bow Avenue has 94, and the upper levels of the Bear Street Parkade are free, all with a nine-hour limit.

Roam Transit is the cheapest way around. Local Banff routes are CAD 2 adult, Route 3 to Canmore is CAD 6, and Route 8X to Lake Louise is CAD 12.50 adult and CAD 6.25 youth or senior, with children under 12 free. Day passes run CAD 5 local, CAD 25 for the Lake Louise Express and CAD 30 system wide.

Seasonality is strict. The Bow Valley Parkway restricts all travel, including cycling and hiking, on a 17 km section east of Johnston Canyon Campground to daylight hours from March 1 to June 25 to protect wildlife. The western section of the Minnewanka Loop road closes to driving from November through May. The Banff Upper Hot Springs charges CAD 19.75 adult, CAD 17.25 senior or youth and CAD 64.25 family, sells tickets in person only on a first-come basis, and rents towels for CAD 3 and swimsuits for CAD 5.
destinations.what_great: The scenery earns the traffic. Moraine Lake in the Valley of the Ten Peaks, Lake Louise under Victoria Glacier and the Icefields Parkway north toward Bow Summit are genuinely among the best mountain views on the continent. Parks Canada infrastructure is excellent, with clean campgrounds from CAD 26.75 a night at Two Jack Main, a real transit network, and free youth entry. Wildlife viewing is legitimate, and the Alpine Start shuttle at 4 a.m. gets you an almost empty lakeshore at sunrise.
destinations.what_ruined: Nearly everything popular now requires a reservation you had to win in April, and losing the 8 a.m. MDT shuttle release means you simply do not see Moraine Lake. CAD 42 to park at Lake Louise, CAD 12 an hour downtown and CAD 17.50 at Sulphur Mountain add up fast on top of accommodation that is among Canada's priciest. The lakeshore lot fills before dawn. Roughly 2.6 million people a year funnel into the Lake Louise corridor, about 70 percent more than in 2010, and it feels like it.
tips: Book the Parks Canada shuttle the moment the 60 percent seat release drops at 8 a.m. MDT exactly two days before your date, because the April 15 launch batch is long gone by summer.

If you visit between June 19 and September 7, 2026, park admission is free under the Canada Strong Pass, so skip the CAD 12.25 daily pass and put the money toward the CAD 12.75 shuttle ticket instead.
Park free at the Train Station lot's 500 stalls rather than paying CAD 12 an hour downtown, and note the nine-hour limit applies.
The Lake Louise lakeshore lot costs CAD 42 per vehicle per day from May 15 to October 12 and is typically full before sunrise, so use the free Park and Ride at 1 Whitehorn Road.
Carry bear spray on every trail and know how to use it, since Banff holds both grizzly and black bears and the Bow Valley montane is prime spring habitat.
Do not plan an early morning or evening drive on the Bow Valley Parkway between March 1 and June 25, when the 17 km section east of Johnston Canyon Campground is open only from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m.

guide.title: Planning Banff Without Losing the Shuttle Lottery: Costs, Months and Access Rules for 2026
guide.summary: A practical breakdown of Banff's reservation systems, real parking and transit costs, month-by-month timing, four sites worth the effort and the wildlife and winter driving risks.
guide.body:
Timing decides the trip. June through August is peak, with long daylight, every trail open and the heaviest congestion, made worse in 2026 by free park admission from June 19 to September 7 under the Canada Strong Pass. Late September into the first week of October is the sweet spot, when the alpine larches turn gold above Moraine Lake for roughly two weeks and the crowds thin on weekdays, though weekends on Larch Valley and Sentinel Pass remain heavily used. May and early June are quiet but many high trails are still under snow and the Bow Valley Parkway carries its wildlife travel restriction from March 1 to June 25. November through April is ski season, cheaper and genuinely beautiful, but the western Minnewanka Loop road is closed to driving from November through May and the Icefields Parkway becomes a serious winter drive.

Getting in from Calgary International Airport is straightforward. Banff Airporter runs the roughly two-hour route at CAD 88.90 one way for adults, CAD 80.01 for seniors and CAD 44.45 for children aged 6 to 17, with round trips at CAD 177.80 adult, and GST is not included in the quoted fare. Renting a car costs more once you add parking, but gives access to the Icefields Parkway and trailheads that no shuttle serves. Whichever you choose, you still need a park pass unless you travel inside the free admission window.

Once there, Roam Transit is the backbone. Local Banff routes cost CAD 2 adult and CAD 1 for youth and seniors, Route 3 to Canmore is CAD 6, and Route 8X to Lake Louise is CAD 12.50 adult and CAD 6.25 youth or senior, with children 12 and under free on everything. A system-wide day pass at CAD 30 pays for itself the moment you make two regional trips. If you drive, park free at the Train Station lot, Bow Avenue or the upper Bear Street Parkade rather than feeding CAD 12 an hour into downtown meters between May 1 and October 31.

Four places deserve your effort. Moraine Lake is the signature view and is reachable only by Parks Canada shuttle, licensed operator or lodge stay, since the road has been closed to private cars year-round since 2023; the shuttle costs CAD 12.75 adult plus a CAD 3.50 online booking fee and runs June 1 to October 12. Lake Louise is served by its own shuttle from May 15 to October 12 and by a Lake Connector between the two lakes every 30 minutes from 7 a.m. to 6 p.m.; going by shuttle avoids the CAD 42 lakeshore parking fee entirely. The Banff Upper Hot Springs on Sulphur Mountain charges CAD 19.75 adult, CAD 17.25 senior or youth and CAD 64.25 family, sells only at the door with no advance booking, and sits in the Sulphur Mountain paid parking pilot zone at CAD 17.50 per vehicle per day from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. between May 15 and October 12. The Icefields Parkway north toward the Columbia Icefield is the one drive worth a full day, and it is free to travel with a valid park pass.

The place worth skipping is the downtown Banff Avenue shopping strip at midday in July, which is where a large share of those 4.5 million annual visitors end up and where the parking meters are most expensive.

On budget, a realistic mid-range day in summer runs about USD 260 to USD 400 per person. Accommodation is the dominant line, with mid-range Banff or Canmore rooms commonly landing between USD 180 and USD 320 a night in high season, which is roughly USD 90 to USD 160 per person on a double. Food adds about USD 60 to USD 90 for a cafe breakfast, a packed lunch and a sit-down dinner. Transport is modest if you use transit, around USD 25 for a system-wide day pass, or far more if you drive and park at Lake Louise, where CAD 42 alone is close to USD 30. Activities such as the hot springs or a shuttle ticket add USD 15 to USD 60. Camping cuts the total sharply: an unserviced site at Two Jack Main is CAD 26.75 and Lake Louise unserviced is CAD 34.00 per night, with a further 25 percent off during the Canada Strong Pass window.

Safety is mostly about wildlife and weather. Banff holds grizzly bears, black bears, wolves, cougars and elk, and Parks Canada is explicit that visitors should travel in groups, make noise, stay alert and carry bear spray they know how to deploy. The montane floor of the Bow Valley greens up first each spring, which is why bears and wolves concentrate there and why the Bow Valley Parkway restriction exists. Elk are dangerous during the autumn rut and the spring calving season and should never be approached in the townsite. On the roads, winter tires or chains are required on the Icefields Parkway in winter conditions, storms arrive early, and mountain weather can turn from sun to sleet within an hour in any month, so carry layers even on a July day hike.

rating_rationale.overall: The landscape and the Parks Canada infrastructure are genuinely world class, but the visitor experience is now mediated by reservation systems and 4.5 million people a year rather than by spontaneity.
rating_rationale.safety: Crime is negligible and the park is professionally managed, but grizzly and black bear encounters, elk during rut and calving, and winter driving on the Icefields Parkway are real hazards that require bear spray and winter tires.
rating_rationale.value: Park entry is only CAD 12.25 a day and free from June 19 to September 7, 2026, yet CAD 42 lakeshore parking, CAD 12 per hour downtown parking and roughly CAD 89 one way from Calgary airport push the real daily cost far higher.

facts_checked.fact: Banff daily entry CAD 12.25 adult, 10.75 senior, 24.50 family/group, youth 17 and under free; Discovery Pass 83.50 / 71.50 / 167.50; Canada Strong Pass free admission and 25 percent off camping June 19 to September 7, 2026
Parks Canada shuttle fees CAD 12.75 adult, 6.00 senior, 4.00 youth; online reservation fee 3.50, phone 5.50
Lake Louise Lakeshore and Fairview Day Use parking CAD 42.00 per day; Sulphur Mountain CAD 17.50 per day; rates apply May 15 to October 12, 2026
Camping rates: Two Jack Main unserviced CAD 26.75, Lake Louise unserviced CAD 34.00, Lake Louise electrical CAD 40.00, Tunnel Mountain full service CAD 47.25

Moraine Lake Road closed to personal vehicles year-round; Park and Ride at 1 Whitehorn Road with free parking; Moraine shuttle June 1 to October 12, 2026 every 30 min 6:30 a.m. to 5 p.m., last return 7:30 p.m., Alpine Start 4 a.m. and 5 a.m.; Lake Louise Lakeshore shuttle May 15 to October 12; Lake Connector June 1 to October 12 every 30 min 7 a.m. to 6 p.m.; reservations launched April 15, 2026 at 8 a.m. MDT with 40 percent of seats and 60 percent released 8 a.m. MDT two days prior
Banff Upper Hot Springs CAD 19.75 adult, 17.25 senior and youth, 64.25 family, extra youth 9.75; locker token 2.00, swimsuit 5.00, towel 3.00; paid parking daily 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. May 15 to October 12

Roam Transit fares: local routes CAD 2 adult / 1 youth-senior, Route 3 Canmore CAD 6 / 3, Route 8X Lake Louise CAD 12.50 / 6.25, children 12 and under free; day passes Banff local 5, Lake Louise Express 25, system wide 30

Town of Banff visitor pay parking CAD 12/hour May 1 to October 31 and CAD 7/hour November 1 to April 30, enforced 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. year-round; free lots at Train Station (500 stalls), Bow Avenue (94 spaces) and upper Bear Street Parkade, nine-hour limit

Bow Valley Parkway travel restriction March 1 to June 25, 8 p.m. to 8 a.m. closure on a 17 km section east of Johnston Canyon Campground; western Minnewanka Loop road closed to driving November to May
Banff Airporter Calgary airport fares CAD 88.90 adult one way, 80.01 senior, 44.45 child 6-17, 177.80 adult round trip, roughly 2 hours, GST not included

Banff National Park recorded about 4.5 million visitors in 2025-26, beating the prior record of about 4.28 million in 2023-24; roughly 2.6 million visit the Lake Louise and Moraine Lake area annually, about 70 percent more than 2010; Sulphur Mountain paid parking is a three-year pilot
Bear spray, group travel and noise are the Parks Canada wildlife recommendations; Bow Valley montane greens up first and concentrates bears, wolves and elk in spring

NOT VERIFIED and therefore omitted from the body: current Banff Gondola adult ticket price (dynamic, no official figure confirmed); the exact winter tire legal requirement wording for the Icefields Parkway (widely reported by operators, not confirmed on an official Parks Canada or Alberta government page); the Larch Valley group-of-four grizzly restriction dates (not present on the Parks Canada closures page fetched)

destinations.name: Hoi An
destinations.summary: UNESCO-listed Vietnamese trading port on the Thu Bon river, known for tailors, lantern nights and heavy autumn flooding. Small, walkable, heavily touristed.
destinations.headline: Hoi An Review: A Genuinely Beautiful Old Town With a Tailor-Shop Hustle and a Flood Season
destinations.body:
Hoi An sits on the Thu Bon river in Quang Nam province, roughly 30 kilometres south of Da Nang, and it was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List on December 4, 1999 as an exceptionally well preserved Southeast Asian trading port of the 15th to 19th centuries. That listing is earned. The Ancient Town core, essentially Tran Phu, Nguyen Thai Hoc and Bach Dang streets, is a dense grid of ochre shophouses, Chinese assembly halls and the Japanese Covered Bridge, known locally as Chua Cau, which reopened in early August 2024 after a two year restoration costing about 20.2 billion Vietnamese dong, funded equally by Quang Nam province and Hoi An city. The rebuilt bridge drew public criticism for looking too new, and visitors expecting a weathered 400 year old structure should reset expectations.

Access to the heritage core runs on a ticket. Foreign visitors pay 120,000 VND, sold at the small yellow booths at the main entrances, valid 24 hours, and the ticket covers entry to five of the 22 listed heritage buildings, with a tear off strip surrendered at each door. Street level enforcement is inconsistent and many people wander the lanes without buying, but every assembly hall, old house and museum checks it at the door.

Arrival is simple. Da Nang International Airport is the gateway, about 45 minutes out. Grab runs roughly 250,000 to 350,000 VND plus a 30,000 VND airport surcharge paid directly to the driver, metered taxis land around 300,000 to 400,000 VND, and prearranged private cars sit at 350,000 to 500,000 VND. Touts working the arrivals hall quote 500,000 to 700,000 VND for the identical trip, so use the app or the official rank and decline everything else.

Timing decides the quality of the visit. February to April is the dependable window, warm and dry with low humidity. May to August stays dry but gets genuinely punishing, with highs approaching 40C. September to November is typhoon and flood season on the central coast, and this is not an abstraction. On October 30, 2025 the Thu Bon crested at 5.62 metres in Hoi An, 14 centimetres above the 1964 record, and water only drained out of the Ancient Town on October 31, leaving thick mud through the shophouses. Bach Dang, Nguyen Thai Hoc and Tran Phu, the exact streets visitors come for, sit lowest and flood first. November 2025 brought a second round of inundation.

The lantern night, Dem Ram Pho Co, falls on the 14th night of each lunar month. Remaining 2026 dates include 27 July, 26 August, 24 September, 23 October, 22 November and 22 December. Motorbikes are cleared from the core around 5:30 pm, electric lighting goes down between roughly 6 and 10 pm, and the candle boat release on the Hoai river peaks between 7 and 9 pm.

Outside town, My Son Sanctuary, the Cham temple complex about 50 kilometres inland, charges 150,000 VND for foreigners, which covers the site museum, the electric shuttle to the temple groups and the traditional dance performance.

The honest caveat is commerce. Tailoring is Hoi An's signature industry and also its best documented problem. Hotel staff, drivers and friendly strangers routinely collect 20 to 40 percent commission for delivering a customer to a particular shop, and that cut comes out of either the price or the garment. Suits quoted under 100 US dollars are typically fused rather than canvassed, and synthetic blends are regularly sold to visitors as mulberry silk. A suit promised in 24 hours is usually a modified off the rack piece from a volume factory. None of this makes Hoi An a bad destination. It makes it a place where the visitor who books a tailor directly, allows three or four days for real fittings, and treats the first quoted number as an opening position ends up with a completely different trip from the one who does not.
destinations.what_great: The heritage core is the real thing, a dense grid of 15th to 19th century shophouses and Chinese assembly halls on Tran Phu and Nguyen Thai Hoc that earned UNESCO listing in December 1999. The scale is walkable and bicycle friendly, with An Bang beach a flat 10 to 20 minute ride away. Lantern nights on the 14th of each lunar month shut the core to traffic and kill the electric lighting between roughly 6 and 10 pm. My Son Sanctuary is a legitimate half day at 150,000 VND.
destinations.what_ruined: Tailoring runs on commission, with hotel staff, drivers and strangers taking 20 to 40 percent for steering you to a shop, money that comes straight out of fabric quality. Suits under 100 dollars are fused, polyester is sold as mulberry silk, and 24 hour turnaround means factory garments. Airport touts quote 500,000 to 700,000 VND for a 300,000 VND ride. September to November is flood season: the Thu Bon hit a record 5.62 metres on October 30, 2025, and flooded again in November. Foreigner pricing is routine.
tips: Buy the Ancient Town ticket at the yellow booths for 120,000 VND; it is valid 24 hours and covers five of the 22 listed heritage buildings via tear-off coupons.
Book a tailor yourself rather than through your hotel or driver, because the referral commission of 20 to 40 percent comes out of your fabric quality or your price.
Aim for February to April, when central Vietnam is warm and dry, and avoid September to November, when the Thu Bon flooded to a record 5.62 metres on October 30, 2025.
From Da Nang airport use Grab at roughly 250,000 to 350,000 VND plus the 30,000 VND airport surcharge, and refuse touts quoting 500,000 to 700,000 VND.
Time your visit to a lantern night on the 14th of the lunar month, with 2026 dates including 24 September and 23 October, when motorbikes clear around 5:30 pm and lights go off from about 6 to 10 pm.
Budget a full 150,000 VND for the My Son Sanctuary ticket, which already covers the site museum, the electric shuttle and the Cham dance performance, and go at opening to beat the heat.

guide.title: Hoi An Practical Guide: Tickets, Da Nang Transfers, Flood Months and Tailor Rules
guide.summary: Month by month timing, real Da Nang airport transfer costs, current Ancient Town and My Son ticket prices, daily budget bands, and how the tailor commission system actually works.
guide.body:
When to go is the single decision that matters most in Hoi An. February through April is the strongest window, with warm days, low humidity and very little rain. May through August is reliably dry but hot, with central Vietnam highs pushing toward 40C, which makes midday walking through the Ancient Town unpleasant and pushes most activity to early morning and after dark. September through November is typhoon and flood season on the central coast and is the period to avoid if the trip is inflexible. The 2025 season was the clearest possible illustration: the Thu Bon river reached 5.62 metres in Hoi An on October 30, 2025, 14 centimetres higher than the 1964 record, floodwater sat in the Ancient Town until October 31, and a second round of flooding hit in early November. December and January are cooler and drier than October and November but still fall inside the wet season, so beach days are unreliable.

Getting in means Da Nang International Airport, roughly 30 kilometres and about 45 minutes north. Grab is the cleanest option at roughly 250,000 to 350,000 VND, with a 30,000 VND airport surcharge handed to the driver on top of the in app fare. A metered taxi from the official rank, Mai Linh among them, generally runs 300,000 to 400,000 VND. A prearranged private transfer or hotel car costs 350,000 to 500,000 VND and is worth it on a late arrival. Freelance touts inside and outside arrivals quote 500,000 to 700,000 VND for the same ride. Decline them, walk to the rank, or open the app.

Getting around town is deliberately easy. The Ancient Town is small and closed to motorbikes during the pedestrian hours, so bicycles, usually free from hotels, cover almost everything including the 10 to 20 minute flat ride east to An Bang and Cua Dai beaches. Grab bikes and cars work for longer runs.

For sites, start with the Ancient Town ticket itself: 120,000 VND for foreigners, sold at yellow booths at the main entrances, valid 24 hours, admitting you to five of the 22 listed heritage buildings via tear off coupons. Spend the coupons on the assembly halls and old merchant houses rather than the smallest museums. The Japanese Covered Bridge, Chua Cau, reopened in August 2024 after a restoration costing about 20.2 billion VND, and it is worth seeing while accepting that the finish is visibly new and has been publicly criticised for exactly that. My Son Sanctuary, about 50 kilometres inland, charges 150,000 VND for foreigners, which includes the site museum, the electric shuttle and the dance show, and is best done on an early morning departure before the heat and the tour buses converge. Try to line the trip up with a lantern night, Dem Ram Pho Co, on the 14th night of the lunar month, with 2026 dates including 27 July, 26 August, 24 September, 23 October, 22 November and 22 December: motorbikes clear around 5:30 pm, electric lights go off from roughly 6 to 10 pm, and candle boats peak on the water between 7 and 9 pm. The one thing worth skipping is any tailor visit arranged by a driver or hotel desk.

On budget, the fixed and verifiable costs are modest. The Ancient Town ticket is 120,000 VND, My Son is 150,000 VND, and the airport transfer is 250,000 to 500,000 VND each way. At roughly 25,000 VND to the US dollar, that is about 5 dollars, 6 dollars and 10 to 20 dollars respectively. Around those numbers, a realistic all in daily figure is about 35 to 55 dollars for a budget traveller in a guesthouse eating at street stalls and local markets, about 70 to 120 dollars for a mid range visitor in a boutique hotel with sit down meals and a day trip, and 200 dollars and up if a tailored wardrobe or a riverside resort enters the picture. Treat clothing as a separate line, not a daily one.

On safety and scams, Hoi An is a low violent crime destination and the practical risks are commercial and environmental rather than personal. The commission system is the core issue: hotel staff, taxi drivers and helpful strangers earn 20 to 40 percent for steering customers to specific tailors, and that money comes out of fabric quality or your price. Suits under 100 dollars are fused rather than canvassed, polyester is sold as mulberry silk, and 24 hour turnaround means a factory made garment altered to fit. Book tailors independently, insist on multiple fittings across three or four days, and pay a deposit rather than the full amount up front. Agree fares before riding with non metered drivers, expect a foreigner price on unlabelled market goods, and during flood months check river conditions before committing to a riverside hotel.

rating_rationale.overall: The Ancient Town is a genuinely intact 15th to 19th century trading port with UNESCO inscription since December 1999, but the tailoring and souvenir economy is intense enough to shape the whole experience.
rating_rationale.safety: Violent crime is not the concern here; the real hazards are commercial pressure and a documented flood season, including the October 30, 2025 Thu Bon crest of 5.62 metres that put the Ancient Town underwater.
rating_rationale.value: Core costs are low, with a 120,000 VND Ancient Town ticket, a 150,000 VND My Son entry and a roughly 250,000 to 350,000 VND Grab from Da Nang airport, though commission-driven tailoring can inflate a trip quickly.

destinations.name: Kyoto
destinations.summary: Japan's former imperial capital, dense with UNESCO temples and machiya streets, now managing record crowds with new taxes, fines and visitor-priced buses.
destinations.headline: Kyoto in 2026: World-Class Heritage, Priced and Policed Like Never Before
destinations.body:
Kyoto rewards travelers who plan around its pressure points rather than walking straight into them. The city holds seventeen UNESCO-listed component sites and drew 10.88 million foreign visitors in 2024, and the strain now shows up in policy. From March 1, 2026 the municipal lodging tax runs on five tiers charged per person, per night: 200 yen for rooms under 6,000 yen, 400 yen from 6,000 to 19,999 yen, 1,000 yen from 20,000 to 49,999 yen, 4,000 yen from 50,000 to 99,999 yen, and 10,000 yen at 100,000 yen and above. That top band is the steepest lodging tax in Japan, and it applies to every guest in the room, not to the room itself.

The geography is simple once you accept it. The eastern strip, Higashiyama, carries the postcard sites: Kiyomizu-dera at 500 yen for adults, Ginkakuji, Nanzen-ji and the Gion entertainment district. The northwest holds Kinkaku-ji, also 500 yen, open 9:00 to 17:00. Central Kyoto has Nijo Castle at 800 yen for the grounds or 1,300 yen including Ninomaru Palace, with Honmaru Palace sold as a separate 1,000 yen ticket that requires advance reservation. South of the station, Fushimi Inari Taisha and its torii corridors are free and open around the clock, which is exactly why the lower paths fill before nine in the morning.

Gion now carries enforcement, not just etiquette signage. Entering the privately owned alleys off Hanamikoji Street carries a 10,000 yen fine, introduced after repeated incidents of visitors harassing geiko and maiko and intruding on residents. The main street itself remains public, and patrols have been expanded to steer people back onto it.

Transport is the daily friction point. City buses run a 230 yen flat fare inside the central zone, but the old 700 yen bus-only day pass was scrapped deliberately, to push visitors off the routes locals commute on. The replacement is the Subway and Bus 1-Day Pass at 1,100 yen for adults and 550 yen for children. Kyoto also runs the EX100 and EX101 sightseeing limited express buses at 500 yen a ride, priced above the standard fare on purpose, reaching Gojozaka for Kiyomizu-dera in roughly ten minutes from Kyoto Station, Gion in seventeen and Ginkakuji in thirty. The city has signalled a resident-priority fare structure, around 200 yen for residents against 350 to 400 yen for everyone else, targeted for rollout by fiscal 2027.

Seasonality is not a preference here, it is the entire trip. Cherry blossoms peak from late March into the first days of April. The tsuyu rainy season runs early June to mid-July. July and August deliver highs above 33C with humidity over 70 percent, and July is also Gion Matsuri month, stacking festival crowds on top of the heat. Autumn foliage peaks from mid-November into early December. Those two colour windows produce the year's highest room rates, and during Golden Week 2026 Kyoto posted the highest average nightly hotel rate of Japan's major cities at 48,900 yen.

An honest visitor should expect a city that is genuinely extraordinary and genuinely oversubscribed at the same time. Temples close earlier than most people assume, commonly around 17:00. Many smaller temples and shrine offices still want cash in yen and will not take a card. Kyoto publishes an official hourly congestion forecast covering Gion, Arashiyama and the Kiyomizu approach, and it is worth reading before leaving the hotel. Travelers who start at 6:30am, favour subway and rail over buses, and space the famous sites across separate mornings get a completely different city from the one experienced by people queueing at eleven.
destinations.what_great: The concentration is unmatched, seventeen UNESCO component sites inside one mid-sized city, with Fushimi Inari free and open at all hours and both Kinkaku-ji and Kiyomizu-dera at just 500 yen for adults. Transit is punctual, and ICOCA, Suica and PASMO IC cards all work on city buses and the subway. Food value stays strong even with a weak yen, with excellent ramen and set lunches well under 2,000 yen. Late-March blossom and mid-November foliage are legitimately world-class.
destinations.what_ruined: Kyoto is now openly rationing visitors. The lodging tax moved to five tiers on March 1, 2026, topping out at 10,000 yen per person per night. The bus-only day pass was abolished to keep tourists off local routes, and tourist-priced express buses cost 500 yen against the 230 yen flat fare. Gion's private alleys carry a 10,000 yen trespass fine. Arashiyama's bamboo grove and the Kiyomizu approach are shoulder to shoulder by mid-morning, and peak-season rooms hit record rates.
tips: Buy the HARUKA one-way discount ticket from Kansai Airport to Kyoto Station at 2,200 yen for adults and 1,100 yen for children, which requires a foreign passport and Temporary Visitor status.
Take the Karasuma or Tozai subway lines wherever possible instead of the 500 yen EX100 tourist express, since the 1,100 yen Subway and Bus 1-Day Pass already covers both.
Reach Fushimi Inari Taisha before 8:00am, because it charges nothing and never closes, so the upper torii paths are effectively empty at dawn.
Budget 1,300 yen for Nijo Castle including Ninomaru Palace, and reserve the separate 1,000 yen Honmaru Palace ticket in advance if you want to see it.
Do not step into the private alleys off Hanamikoji Street in Gion, where trespassing now carries a 10,000 yen fine.
Check Kyoto's official hourly congestion forecast at global.kyoto.travel before committing a morning to Arashiyama or the Kiyomizu approach.

guide.title: Kyoto Trip Planner 2026: Costs, Crowds, Transit and the New Lodging Tax
guide.summary: A month-by-month, fare-by-fare planning guide to Kyoto in 2026, covering airport transfers, the restructured lodging tax, temple entry fees, realistic daily budgets and where the crowds actually sit.
guide.body:
January and February are the cheapest and emptiest months in Kyoto, cold but comparatively dry, with business hotel rates falling well below spring levels. March warms fast, and cherry blossoms usually peak from late March into the first days of April, which is the single most crowded and most expensive window of the year alongside November. Late April and early May bring Golden Week, when Kyoto recorded the highest average hotel rate of any major Japanese city in 2026 at 48,900 yen a night. The tsuyu rainy season settles in from early June to mid-July. July and August are punishing, with highs above 33C and humidity over 70 percent, and July is also Gion Matsuri month. September carries typhoon risk, with Kyoto typically catching one to three storms in the late-August to September stretch. October is the quiet compromise nobody talks about. Autumn foliage peaks from mid-November into early December, spectacular and completely mobbed.

Kansai International is the main gateway, roughly 75 to 90 minutes from Kyoto. The JR HARUKA limited express runs direct to Kyoto Station, and foreign passport holders entering Japan on Temporary Visitor status can buy a discounted one-way HARUKA ticket for 2,200 yen for adults and 1,100 yen for children, materially cheaper than paying the standard base fare plus limited express charge. Buy it before clearing the JR gates and carry your passport, since it must be presented at purchase. Limousine buses also run from both Kansai and Osaka Itami. Taxis from Kansai are extremely expensive and only make sense for a group arriving after the last train, which departs Kansai for Kyoto around 10:16pm.

Kyoto is a rail city pretending to be a bus city. City buses charge a 230 yen flat fare in the central zone and are the single most congested part of the network, which is why the old 700 yen bus-only day pass was deliberately abolished. The Subway and Bus 1-Day Pass at 1,100 yen for adults and 550 yen for children covers both municipal subway lines, most city bus routes, and the EX100 and EX101 sightseeing express buses that otherwise cost 500 yen per ride. ICOCA, Suica and PASMO cards work across buses and subway. Rented bicycles are genuinely the fastest way across the flat central grid, and walking Higashiyama end to end beats queueing at Kyoto Station bus stop D, which regularly fills to capacity and leaves people waiting for the next bus.

Fushimi Inari Taisha is free, open at all hours, and the only major site you can realistically have to yourself if you arrive by 8:00am; walk past the first dense torii tunnel and the crowd thins dramatically. Kinkaku-ji costs 500 yen and is open 9:00 to 17:00, and it is a single fixed viewing loop of maybe twenty-five minutes, so treat it as a stop rather than a morning. Kiyomizu-dera is 500 yen, and the approach streets of Sannenzaka and Ninenzaka are both the real attraction and the real crush. Nijo Castle is 800 yen for the grounds or 1,300 yen with Ninomaru Palace and its nightingale floors, with Honmaru Palace a separate 1,000 yen reserved ticket and the painting gallery another 100 yen. Arashiyama's bamboo grove is free and, honestly, close to a skip after 9:00am, since it is a short path; nearby Tenryu-ji and the wider Sagano area repay the trip far better. Gion is worth an evening on Hanamikoji Street itself, never in the side alleys.

A realistic mid-range day in Kyoto in 2026 runs roughly 130 to 200 USD per person. Budget accommodation in a business hotel typically sits around 60 to 110 USD in shoulder season and considerably more in April and November, with reported peak-season business hotel rates climbing from about 10,000 yen to 25,000 yen a night, plus the per-person lodging tax of 200 to 1,000 yen at those price points. Food is the bargain of the trip, with ramen or a set lunch around 6 to 10 USD and a good dinner 20 to 35 USD. Transit costs about 8 USD a day with the 1,100 yen pass. Temple entries run 3 to 9 USD each, and three a day is plenty. Backpackers can hold 70 to 90 USD a day with hostels and convenience store meals. Anyone booking a ryokan with kaiseki should expect 300 USD and up, and the top lodging tax band of 10,000 yen per person per night applies once the nightly rate reaches 100,000 yen.

Kyoto is one of the safest large cities anywhere and violent crime against visitors is rare, so the risks are procedural rather than personal. Carry cash, because smaller temples, shrine offices and older restaurants still refuse cards, and IC card top-ups need yen. Avoid unlicensed geisha-experience touts, and never photograph geiko or maiko without explicit consent, which now carries real enforcement in Gion alongside the 10,000 yen fine for entering the private alleys off Hanamikoji. Expect luggage friction on crowded buses at peak times. Above all, check the city's official congestion forecast before setting out, since it is published specifically because the crowd problem is real.

rating_rationale.overall: The concentration of intact heritage is close to unmatched, with seventeen UNESCO-listed component sites and free landmark shrines like Fushimi Inari inside one mid-sized city.
rating_rationale.safety: Violent crime against visitors is rare and transit is orderly; the practical risks are cash-only venues and crowd crush at peak sites rather than personal danger.
rating_rationale.value: Temple entry and food remain cheap, but the March 2026 lodging tax restructure, record peak-season room rates such as the 48,900 yen Golden Week average, and 500 yen tourist-priced express buses push the total cost up sharply.

destinations.name: Lisbon
destinations.summary: Portugal's Atlantic capital, hilly and tiled, still good value in Western Europe, now under short-term rental caps, tuk-tuk bans and heavy pickpocket pressure.
destinations.headline: Lisbon in 2026: Still Excellent Value, Now Openly Fighting Its Own Tourism
destinations.body:
Lisbon rewards travelers who accept that the city is having an argument with tourism in public. Portugal's capital is built across seven hills above the Tagus, and the sightseeing map is small: Baixa, the flat grid rebuilt after the 1755 earthquake, Alfama, the Moorish-era lane network below Castelo de Sao Jorge, Bairro Alto and Principe Real on the ridge above, and Belem, a few kilometres west, where the monuments are.

Costs remain moderate by Western European standards, but they are moving. The municipal tourist tax is 4 euros per person per night for guests aged 13 and over, capped at seven nights per stay. Transport fares rose on 1 January 2026: a single Carris or Metro journey on a navegante card is 1.90 euros, zapping pay-as-you-go is 1.72 euros, and the reusable navegante occasional card itself costs 0.50 euros. The 24-hour Carris and Metro ticket is 7.25 euros, rising to 10.35 euros including Transtejo ferries and 11.40 euros including CP trains, which is the version you want for Sintra or Cascais. Buying onboard is punished: 2.30 euros on a bus, 3.30 euros on a tram, 4.30 euros for a funicular such as the Gloria or the Bica, and 6.20 euros for the Santa Justa Lift.

The headline sites are straightforward. Castelo de Sao Jorge charges 17 euros for adults, 8.50 euros for ages 13 to 25, 14 euros for over-65s, 12 euros for visitors with specific needs, and is free for under-12s. In Belem, the Jeronimos Monastery cloister is ticketed while the adjoining church of Santa Maria de Belem is free to enter. The Torre de Belem reopened on 26 May 2026 after roughly a year of restoration and now runs strict timed entry, capped at 60 visitors every 30 minutes and 900 a day, Tuesday to Sunday, 09:30 to 17:30 with last entry at 17:00. That cap is a genuine constraint rather than a formality, and walk-up availability in summer is unlikely.

The overtourism response here is aimed at housing and streets rather than purely at your wallet. Lisbon's short-term rental containment rules, approved on 27 November 2025 and effective from 6 December 2025, block new Alojamento Local registrations in any parish where AL units already make up 10 percent or more of permanent housing, with an intermediate authorisation regime between 5 and 10 percent. That covers most of the historic centre, including Alfama, Baixa, Bairro Alto and Principe Real, and in Santa Maria Maior the AL density sits near 67 percent. Tuk-tuks are now banned from 337 streets after a further 67 were added on 1 April, with EMEL reinforcing enforcement with 62 inspectors alongside the municipal police. Housing marches moved through Lisbon in March 2026 under slogans tying tourism, aviation and rents together. Visitors are not the target, but they are unmistakably the subject.

Weather is the quiet advantage. Lisbon is comfortable across a far longer season than most European capitals, with mild wet winters and dry hot summers. July and August are the busiest and hottest stretch, and the shoulder months on either side are better in almost every respect.

An honest visitor should expect steep gradients and slick calcada paving that punishes bad shoes, and should expect the crowds to concentrate hard on three things: tram 28E, the Belem monuments, and the Alfama viewpoints at sunset. Everything else is comparatively calm. Restaurants outside the tourist grid remain good value, and the couvert of bread, olives and cheese placed on your table is chargeable rather than free, and can be sent back untouched.
destinations.what_great: Lisbon delivers unusually high quality for the money, with a compact core walkable in a day and genuinely good food a street or two off the tourist grid. Transit is cheap and integrated, 1.90 euros a journey on a navegante card and 7.25 euros for 24 hours across Carris and Metro. The Atlantic light and the Tagus viewpoints are exceptional. Belem's monuments are UNESCO-listed, and the restored Torre de Belem reopened in May 2026. Winters are mild enough to visit year-round.
destinations.what_ruined: Tram 28E is documented as the most pickpocketed transit line in Portugal, and the Baixa and Rossio grid runs thick with fake-drug approaches used purely as cover for lifting wallets, the product usually being crushed leaves rather than anything illegal. Belem Tower is capped at 900 visitors a day, so unplanned visits simply fail. Onboard fares are punitive, 3.30 euros on a tram against 1.90 on a card. Rental caps and a 337-street tuk-tuk ban reflect real local anger.
tips: Buy the reusable navegante occasional card for 0.50 euros at any Metro machine and load zapping at 1.72 euros a journey instead of paying 3.30 euros onboard a tram.
Take the Metro Red Line from Humberto Delgado Airport for 1.90 euros rather than a taxi, which typically runs 15 to 20 euros plus a 1.60 euro luggage surcharge.
Book a Torre de Belem timed slot ahead of arrival, because entry is capped at 60 people per 30 minutes and 900 per day, Tuesday to Sunday, with last entry at 17:00.
Ride tram 12E or a parallel bus rather than 28E, which is the most pickpocketed transit line in the country.
Budget 17 euros for adult entry to Castelo de Sao Jorge, and buy only through the castle's authorised BOL ticketing page or the on-site ticket office.
Decline the couvert of bread and olives if you do not want it, since it is charged to the bill, and expect the 4 euro per person nightly tourist tax at checkout.

guide.title: Lisbon Planning Guide 2026: Fares, Timed Entry, Real Budgets and Scam Hotspots
guide.summary: How to plan Lisbon in 2026: month-by-month timing, airport transfers, the January fare rise, which monuments now need booking, honest daily budgets, and where pickpockets actually work.
guide.body:
Lisbon has one of the longest usable seasons of any Western European capital, which means the month you choose changes cost far more than it changes comfort. March through early June is the sweet spot, warm and dry enough for the hills without August heat, and mid-September through October repeats it with warmer ocean water. July and August are the hottest and by far the most crowded, with cruise arrivals stacking on top of the summer flow, and this is when Belem and tram 28E become genuinely unpleasant. June brings the Santo Antonio street festivities that fill Alfama and Graca with grilled sardines and improvised bars. November through February is wet but mild, and the cheapest time to be here by a wide margin, with the caveat that hill streets get slick in rain.

Humberto Delgado Airport sits inside the city, roughly 20 to 30 minutes from the centre. The Metro Red Line runs directly from the terminal for 1.90 euros on a navegante card, or 1.72 euros with zapping, with the reusable card itself costing 0.50 euros. The old Aerobus was permanently discontinued in 2022, so ignore any guide still listing it. A taxi to central Lisbon typically runs 15 to 20 euros plus a 1.60 euro luggage surcharge; the 2026 urban meter rate is 0.96 euros per kilometre with a 3.32 euro minimum, rising to 1.21 euros per kilometre and a 3.98 euro minimum between 21:00 and 06:00 and on weekends and public holidays. Never accept a ride from anyone who approaches you inside the terminal building, since those drivers are unregulated and charge whatever they like.

Getting around is cheap if you buy correctly and expensive if you do not. Load the navegante card and every Metro, Carris bus and tram journey costs 1.90 euros, or 1.72 with zapping. Pay the driver instead and a bus is 2.30 euros and a tram 3.30 euros. The 24-hour Carris and Metro ticket is 7.25 euros, the version including Transtejo ferries to Cacilhas is 10.35 euros, and the version including CP trains, which is what covers Sintra and Cascais, is 11.40 euros. The funiculars, Gloria, Bica and Lavra, are 4.30 euros for up to two journeys, and the Santa Justa Lift is 6.20 euros, which is poor value against the queue it usually carries. Walking is the default in the centre, but the gradients are real and the polished calcada stone is slippery in rain.

Castelo de Sao Jorge is the classic first stop at 17 euros for adults, 8.50 euros for ages 13 to 25, 14 euros for over-65s and free under 12, with the caveat that its main product is a view you can get free from Miradouro da Senhora do Monte or Miradouro da Graca. In Belem, the Jeronimos Monastery cloister is the genuinely unmissable interior in the city, and the adjacent church of Santa Maria de Belem is free. The Torre de Belem reopened on 26 May 2026 after a roughly one million euro restoration and now runs timed entry capped at 60 visitors per 30 minutes and 900 per day, Tuesday to Sunday 09:30 to 17:30 with last entry at 17:00, bookable online or at the Jeronimos ticket office; without a slot, do not plan on getting in. Alfama itself is the free attraction, best walked downhill from the castle in the early morning before the tour groups. Sintra, forty minutes by CP train, deserves a full day rather than an afternoon, and its palaces are ticketed separately from anything in Lisbon.

A realistic mid-range day in Lisbon in 2026 lands around 110 to 170 USD per person. Accommodation is the swing factor, with mid-range hotels typically 90 to 160 USD in shoulder season and higher in summer, plus the 4 euro per person nightly tourist tax for anyone 13 or over, capped at seven nights. Transit is 7.25 euros for a 24-hour Carris and Metro pass or roughly 4 to 6 euros a day on pay-as-you-go. Attractions run 8 to 20 euros each, with the 17 euro castle at the top of the normal range, so two paid sites a day is a sensible ceiling. Food is where Lisbon still wins, with coffee and pastries costing a couple of euros and a neighbourhood lunch a fraction of what the same meal costs in Paris or Amsterdam. Budget travellers can hold 55 to 80 USD a day using hostels, set lunches and the pass.

On safety, Lisbon ranks among Europe's safest capitals for violent crime, and the risk you actually face is theft. Tram 28E is the highest-risk single location in the city and the most pickpocketed transit line in Portugal, worked by teams who block doors and create distractions in the crush; take 12E or a parallel bus instead, or ride 28E at 7am from the Martim Moniz terminus with your bag in front of you. Rossio and the Baixa grid are the second hotspot, where strangers offering drugs are almost never selling drugs, since the product is typically crushed leaves and the transaction is a pretext for a second person to lift your phone. Petition clipboards and shell games work the same squares. Keep nothing in a back pocket, use a zipped cross-body bag, and treat any unsolicited approach in Baixa as the opening move of a theft rather than a conversation.

rating_rationale.overall: A compact, walkable capital with UNESCO monuments in Belem, exceptional viewpoints and a long usable season, held back only by how concentrated the crowding has become.
rating_rationale.safety: Violent crime is low by European capital standards, but tram 28E is documented as the most pickpocketed transit line in Portugal and the Baixa and Rossio grid is a scam hotspot.
rating_rationale.value: Transit is cheap at 1.90 euros a journey and 7.25 euros for 24 hours, and food outside the tourist grid is strong value, though the 4 euro nightly tourist tax and 17 euro castle entry are creeping upward.

destinations.name: Marrakech
destinations.summary: Historic imperial city in central Morocco built around a walled UNESCO medina, the Jemaa el-Fna square, palace museums and the modern Gueliz district.
destinations.headline: Marrakech Delivers World-Class Monuments For 100 Dirhams, And Constant Hassle For Free
destinations.body:
Marrakech sits inland at the foot of the High Atlas, and almost everything visitors come for is packed inside the ochre walls of the medina, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The gravitational centre is Jemaa el-Fna, the square that fills every evening with food stalls, musicians and crowds, and which UNESCO separately recognises for its oral storytelling tradition. Around it run the souks of the Mouassine quarter, the Kasbah district to the south, and the Mellah, the old Jewish quarter, to the southeast. Outside the walls to the northwest lies Gueliz, the French-built ville nouvelle, which is flatter, greener, easier to walk and where most modern restaurants, galleries and shops sit.

Entry fees are standardised and modest. The Ministry of Culture charges foreign adults 100 dirhams each at Bahia Palace, El Badi Palace and the Saadian Tombs, with foreign children aged 7 to 13 paying 50 dirhams. Moroccan nationals and residents pay 30 dirhams and enter free every Friday and on the first day of national and religious holidays, so foreign visitors should not expect a free day. The Ben Youssef Madrasa, restored and reopened, charges foreign adults 50 dirhams, Moroccan adults 20 dirhams, children under 12 ten dirhams and groups of 21 or more 30 dirhams per head, opening 9am to 7pm daily and closing at 4.30pm during Ramadan. Le Jardin Secret in the Mouassine charges 100 dirhams to enter plus 40 dirhams for the tower, with tickets sold on site in dirhams or euros. Jardin Majorelle and the Musée Yves Saint Laurent Marrakech sell tickets online only through tickets.jardinmajorelle.com; the garden opens daily 8am to 6.30pm with last entry at 6pm, the Pierre Bergé Museum of Berber Arts 8.30am to 6pm, and the YSL museum 10am to 6.30pm every day except Wednesday. Koutoubia Mosque, the twelfth-century landmark beside the square, is closed to non-Muslims, so the visit is the exterior and gardens only.

Season matters more here than in most cities. Average highs reach 37.7C in July and 37.4C in August against 19.2C in January, and annual rainfall is only around 280mm, concentrated from February to April and again in November. March and April, and late September to mid-November, are the sensible windows. Anyone arriving in July or August should plan around the heat, front-loading sightseeing before 11am and treating mid-afternoon as indoor time.

The magnitude 6.9 Al Haouz earthquake of 8 September 2023 killed at least 2,960 people and damaged heritage in the medina, most visibly cutting the minaret of the Kharbouch mosque on Jemaa el-Fna roughly in half. The Mellah, with its older houses and narrow lanes, was the worst affected quarter inside the walls. Emergency consolidation and restoration works at sites including Koutoubia and Bahia Palace have been funded and were reviewed by UNESCO in 2024, and the main monuments are operating normally, but scaffolding and closed side streets remain part of the picture.

Getting in and around is straightforward. Menara Airport sits roughly six kilometres from the medina, the L19 shuttle bus runs into the centre for a low flat fare paid on board, and petit taxis are legally required to run meters even though many drivers will not. Most nationalities including US, UK, EU, Canadian and Australian passport holders enter visa free for up to 90 days. Bring cash, expect to be approached constantly, and treat every unsolicited offer of directions as a paid service.
destinations.what_great: The monument density is remarkable: Bahia Palace, El Badi, the Saadian Tombs and Ben Youssef Madrasa are all walkable from Jemaa el-Fna and cost 50 to 100 dirhams each for foreigners. Jemaa el-Fna at dusk is a genuine living tradition, not a staged show. Gueliz offers a calm, tree-lined counterweight to the souks. Food is cheap and excellent, the light is superb for photography from October to April, and the High Atlas begins less than an hour away.
destinations.what_ruined: The hassle is relentless and wears people down. Faux guides attach themselves near medina gates, walk you somewhere you did not ask to go, then demand payment. Henna women in and around Jemaa el-Fna start an unrequested design on your hand and then insist on a large fee. Photo handlers with monkeys and snakes charge aggressively after the shot. Petit taxi drivers routinely refuse the legally required meter and quote inflated flat rates, worse after dark. Souk haggling turns exhausting by day three, and July and August highs near 38C flatten any itinerary.
tips: Buy Jardin Majorelle and Musée Yves Saint Laurent tickets in advance at tickets.jardinmajorelle.com, because they are sold online only and the garden's last entry is 6pm.
Budget 100 dirhams per foreign adult for each of Bahia Palace, El Badi Palace and the Saadian Tombs, and carry small notes since ticket desks handle cash poorly.
Ben Youssef Madrasa costs foreign adults 50 dirhams and opens 9am to 7pm, but closes at 4.30pm during Ramadan, so check the dates before planning a late visit.
Insist on the meter in a petit taxi, and if the driver claims it is broken, get out and take the next one, as meters are legally required.
Visit between March and April or late September and mid-November, because July and August average highs of 37.7C make midday sightseeing genuinely unsafe for many people.
Never accept an unsolicited offer of directions inside the medina, and if hassle turns aggressive, ask for the Brigade Touristique, who patrol Jemaa el-Fna.

guide.title: Marrakech Practical Guide: Real Entry Fees, Airport Transfers, Seasons And Scam Defence
guide.summary: Month-by-month timing, Menara Airport transfers, medina navigation, verified monument fees, a realistic daily budget in US dollars, and a blunt section on the city's documented scams.
guide.body:
Timing is the single biggest decision. Marrakech averages highs of 37.7C in July and 37.4C in August, with lows still above 20C, and summers regularly push past 45C during heat events. January is the coolest month, averaging a high of 19.2C and a low of 5.9C, and riads without proper heating get genuinely cold at night. Rain is scarce, around 280mm a year, falling mostly February to April and again in November. The best windows are March and April, and late September to mid-November, when daytime highs sit in the mid twenties to low thirties. December to February works well for anyone who prefers cool weather and lower crowds, provided you pack layers for evenings. Ramadan shifts opening hours across the city, with Ben Youssef Madrasa closing at 4.30pm instead of 7pm, and many restaurants adjusting service.

Menara Airport is about six kilometres from the medina, which is a fifteen to twenty minute drive in normal traffic. The ALSA L19 shuttle bus runs from outside the terminal into the centre, stopping near Koutoubia for the walk to Jemaa el-Fna and continuing through Gueliz, with tickets bought from the driver in cash for a low flat fare. Petit taxis are the alternative and are the usual friction point: drivers at the airport habitually quote a flat rate rather than run the meter, and night rates are legitimately higher than day rates under the regional tariff system. Agree the number before the doors close, or walk to the road outside the terminal perimeter and flag a taxi that is already running its meter. Pre-booked riad transfers cost more than a taxi but remove the argument entirely, which is worth it on a late arrival with luggage.

Inside the walls, walking is the only real option, because the medina lanes are too narrow and too crowded for anything else. Distances are short, but navigation is the hard part, so download offline maps before arriving and accept that you will still get lost. Petit taxis connect the medina to Gueliz, the Menara Gardens and the Palmeraie quickly and cheaply when metered. Horse-drawn caleches operate from the square on fixed municipal routes and are a tourist ride rather than transport. The ONCF rail station in Gueliz links Marrakech to Casablanca and Rabat on the standard network, which is the practical way to add a second city.

On what to see, the Ministry of Culture sites are the backbone. Bahia Palace, an 8-hectare nineteenth-century complex of courtyards and painted ceilings, charges foreign adults 100 dirhams. El Badi Palace, the ruined Saadian showpiece, is the same 100 dirhams and rewards a slower visit for its scale and the storks nesting on the walls. The Saadian Tombs, the sixteenth-century necropolis rediscovered in 1917, also cost 100 dirhams and get very congested, so arrive at opening. Ben Youssef Madrasa charges foreign adults 50 dirhams and is arguably the best value single monument in the city. Le Jardin Secret costs 100 dirhams plus 40 dirhams for the tower and is a genuinely calm break in the middle of the souks. Jardin Majorelle is the city's most heavily booked attraction and sells tickets online only, so treat it as a fixed-time appointment rather than a walk-up. Koutoubia Mosque is closed to non-Muslims and should be treated as an exterior landmark, not a visit.

On budget, expect a lean independent trip to run roughly 55 to 85 US dollars per person per day, covering a simple riad room, street and cafe eating, local transport and one paid monument. A comfortable mid-range trip runs roughly 130 to 220 dollars per day, with a good riad, sit-down restaurant meals, two or three monuments and a taxi habit. Upper-end riads, private guides and day trips into the Atlas or the Agafay desert push past 350 dollars per day easily. Monuments themselves are a minor line item at 50 to 100 dirhams each. Accommodation and guided excursions are what move the total. Note that a per-person, per-night accommodation tax is added at checkout by most riads and hotels and is usually collected in cash, so keep dirhams aside for it.

On safety, Marrakech is not a violent-crime problem for visitors, it is a persistence problem. The faux guide pattern is the defining one: someone approaches near a medina gate, says the way ahead is closed or that they are heading the same direction, leads you somewhere, then demands payment. Decline firmly and keep walking. Henna applicators in and around Jemaa el-Fna will begin a design on an unsuspecting hand and then demand a large fee, and photo handlers with monkeys and snakes work the same way. Anything placed on you or started for you is a bill. Petit taxi meter refusal is the most common daily annoyance and the fix is simply to take a different taxi. The Brigade Touristique patrols the main square and tourist areas and takes harassment complaints seriously, so naming them usually ends a confrontation. Carry cash in small denominations, keep valuables out of open bags in crowded souk lanes, and be aware that some medina streets damaged in the 2023 earthquake remain closed or under scaffolding.

rating_rationale.overall: The concentration of genuinely first-rank monuments inside a compact walled medina, most charging only 50 to 100 dirhams for foreign adults, is exceptional value for a city of this scale.
rating_rationale.safety: Violent crime against visitors is rare and the Brigade Touristique is active around Jemaa el-Fna, but persistent commission-driven hassle, faux guides and taxi meter refusal are documented and constant.
rating_rationale.value: Ministry of Culture monuments cost 100 dirhams for foreign adults, the airport bus runs about 30 dirhams and food is cheap, so daily costs stay low even when accommodation is not.

destinations.name: Oaxaca
destinations.summary: Colonial capital and Zapotec heartland in southern Mexico, known for mezcal, mole, Monte Alban, and two festival peaks that overwhelm the city's capacity.
destinations.headline: Oaxaca Review: World-Class Food and Ruins, Priced and Crowded to the Limit in Festival Season
destinations.body:
Oaxaca de Juarez is the capital of Oaxaca state in southern Mexico, and the city centre together with the archaeological site of Monte Alban was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1987. The grid of the Centro Historico is Spanish colonial town planning built low and heavy for an earthquake zone, anchored by the Zocalo, the pedestrian Andador Macedonio Alcala, and the Templo de Santo Domingo de Guzman. The former convent beside the church holds the Museo de las Culturas de Oaxaca, which INAH lists at 210 pesos general admission and 105 pesos for Mexican nationals and residents, open Tuesday to Saturday 10:00 to 17:30 with last entry at 17:00 and Sunday 10:00 to 15:30, closed Mondays.

Monte Alban itself sits on a levelled ridge about 10 kilometres from the centre and is open daily 8:00 to 17:00 with last admission at 16:00. INAH's official rate is 210 pesos for general admission and 105 pesos for Mexican nationals and residents, under the national fee schedule effective January 1, 2026. Sundays are free for Mexicans and legal residents, which also makes Sunday the most crowded day. Mitla, about 45 kilometres southeast and famous for its geometric stone fretwork, is listed by INAH at 105 pesos with hours of 8:00 to 17:00 and last access at 16:30.

The Centro Historico carries the museums, the Zocalo and the highest prices. Jalatlaco, a few blocks northeast, is the quieter cobbled quarter with murals and smaller kitchens. Xochimilco, uphill to the north, is residential and calm. Reforma is where longer stay visitors and remote workers concentrate.

Arrival is through Oaxaca International Airport, roughly nine kilometres south of the centre. The official airport colectivo runs 135 pesos to Zone 1 covering the Centro Historico and 210 pesos to Zone 2, while a taxi especial is 490 pesos to Zone 1 and 715 pesos to Zone 2, with rates posted at the terminal booth. Uber and DiDi remain effectively banned across Oaxaca state as of 2026, so ride hailing is not the fallback it is elsewhere in Mexico.

The calendar is unusually consequential. The dry season runs roughly October to April and the rains fall May to September as afternoon downpours, with June the wettest month. Two events dominate. The Guelaguetza culminates on the Lunes del Cerro at the Auditorio Guelaguetza on Cerro del Fortin, falling on Monday July 20 and Monday July 27 in 2026, with reported prices of 1,476 pesos presale and 1,632 pesos regular for Palco A and 1,166 presale and 1,321 regular for Palco B, while Palcos C and D are free on a first arrival basis. Dia de Muertos runs October 31 to November 2, with the vigils at the Santa Cruz Xoxocotlan cemetery about five kilometres from the centre drawing the largest crowds.

Both peaks come with a real cost. Day of the Dead accommodation is reported at 50 to 80 percent above baseline rates with three to five night minimums, and properties commonly sell out by August or September. Tourism to Oaxaca has risen roughly 77 percent since 2020, and residents have organised protests over short term rentals, housing displacement and water allocation, with reports of neighbourhoods going weeks without running water while hotels stayed supplied. Visitors are arriving into an active local dispute, not a neutral backdrop.

The food and mezcal justify the trip on their own. Mercado 20 de Noviembre and Mercado Benito Juarez sit side by side downtown, and the Sunday market at Tlacolula is worth the drive. On mezcal, the honest warning is that cheap tours are the problem: operators charging under about 30 US dollars per person typically recoup the difference through commission on bottle sales, which forces the palenques in and around Santiago Matatlan to hard sell. Paying more for a smaller group that pays the producer properly is the difference between a distillery visit and a sales pitch.
destinations.what_great: Monte Alban is a levelled mountaintop city with 1,500 years of Olmec, Zapotec and Mixtec occupation, open 8:00 to 17:00 for 210 pesos, and it is 10 kilometres from a colonial grid that shares the same 1987 UNESCO inscription. The Museo de las Culturas at Santo Domingo holds the Tomb 7 Mixtec gold. Mercado 20 de Noviembre and the Sunday Tlacolula market are working markets, not displays. Mitla's stone fretwork at 105 pesos has no equivalent anywhere in Mexico.
destinations.what_ruined: Festival season breaks the city. Day of the Dead lodging is reported at 50 to 80 percent above baseline with three to five night minimums and properties sold out by August. Guelaguetza box seats run to 1,632 pesos. Tourism is up roughly 77 percent since 2020, and residents have protested short-term rentals, displacement and water allocation, with reports of neighbourhoods dry for weeks while hotels stayed supplied. Mezcal tours under 30 dollars recoup costs by pressuring palenques into hard selling. Hierve el Agua closes unpredictably.
tips: Arrive at Monte Alban at the 8:00 opening, since the site closes at 17:00 with last admission at 16:00 and there is almost no shade on the Gran Plaza.
Skip Monte Alban on Sunday if you want space, because free entry for Mexicans and residents that day makes it the most crowded of the week.
Take the official airport colectivo at 135 pesos to the Centro Historico instead of the 490 peso taxi especial, since both are booked at the same posted-rate terminal booth.
Do not plan on Uber or DiDi, which remain effectively banned across Oaxaca state in 2026, and agree a fare with street taxis before getting in.
Buy Guelaguetza tickets only through SuperBoletos, the sole authorised online vendor, or queue for the free Palco C and D seating on the Lunes del Cerro dates of 20 and 27 July 2026.
Carry cash for Hierve el Agua, where entry is cash only at roughly 50 to 70 pesos plus a community fee and there are no ATMs at the site, and confirm within a week that it has not closed.

guide.title: Oaxaca Practical Guide: OAX Transfers, Monte Alban Fees, Festival Months and Real Budgets
guide.summary: Month by month timing around Guelaguetza and Day of the Dead, official airport transfer rates, current INAH site fees, honest daily budget bands, and the scams worth knowing.
guide.body:
Oaxaca has a dry season from roughly October to April and a rainy season from May to September. The rains are afternoon events rather than all day washouts, with most mornings clear, and June is the wettest month. The most comfortable stretch is late January into early March, when the heat has not built and humidity is at its lowest. May, June, September and early October are the cheapest and quietest months, with lower hotel rates and thinner crowds, at the cost of daily afternoon storms. The two dates that will define your trip and your budget are the Guelaguetza, whose Lunes del Cerro performances at the Auditorio Guelaguetza on Cerro del Fortin fall on Monday July 20 and Monday July 27 in 2026, and Dia de Muertos, which runs October 31 to November 2. Both fill the city. Day of the Dead in particular is reported to push accommodation 50 to 80 percent above baseline with three to five night minimums, with many properties sold out by August or September, so book those months ahead or deliberately choose different dates.

Getting in means Oaxaca International Airport, about nine kilometres south of the centre and 20 to 30 minutes by road. The official airport colectivo, a shared van booked at the terminal booth with posted rates, costs 135 pesos to Zone 1, which covers the Centro Historico, and 210 pesos to Zone 2 for outlying neighbourhoods like San Felipe del Agua. A private taxi especial from the same booth is 490 pesos to Zone 1 and 715 pesos to Zone 2. Card payment is accepted at the booth. Plan for the absence of ride hailing: Uber and DiDi remain effectively banned across Oaxaca state as of 2026, so street taxis, the colectivo system and walking are the real options.

Getting around the city is overwhelmingly on foot. The Centro Historico grid is compact and the Andador Macedonio Alcala is pedestrianised. For the valley, colectivos and second class buses leave regularly for Mitla, Tlacolula and Santa Maria del Tule, and a hired driver for a full day of valley sites is the efficient alternative to a group tour.

For sites, Monte Alban is the anchor. Open daily 8:00 to 17:00 with last entry at 16:00, admission is 210 pesos general and 105 pesos for Mexican nationals and residents under the INAH schedule that took effect January 1, 2026. Sundays are free for Mexicans and residents, which is exactly why Sunday is the worst day to go for space and quiet. Get there at opening for shade and empty plazas. The Museo de las Culturas de Oaxaca in the former convent at Santo Domingo is 210 pesos general and 105 for nationals and residents, open Tuesday to Saturday 10:00 to 17:30 with last entry at 17:00 and Sunday 10:00 to 15:30, closed Mondays, and the Tomb 7 Mixtec goldwork inside is genuinely world class. Mitla, 45 kilometres southeast, is listed by INAH at 105 pesos, 8:00 to 17:00, last access 16:30, and its geometric stone mosaics are unlike anything else in Mexico. Hierve el Agua, the mineral terraces east of the city, is the one to approach with caution: entry is cash only and typically quoted in the range of 50 to 70 pesos plus a small community fee, but the site has closed unpredictably during an ongoing dispute between the co-managing communities, so confirm access within a week of going. The Sunday market at Tlacolula is worth building a day around. The thing to skip is any bargain mezcal tour priced under about 30 US dollars per person.

On budget, the verifiable line items are the airport transfer at 135 to 490 pesos, Monte Alban at 210 pesos, the Santo Domingo museum at 210 pesos and Mitla at 105 pesos. At roughly 18 pesos to the US dollar, that is about 7 to 27 dollars, 12 dollars, 12 dollars and 6 dollars. Building outward from those, a realistic daily total is about 45 to 65 dollars for a budget traveller in a hostel or guesthouse eating at Mercado 20 de Noviembre, about 90 to 150 dollars for a mid range visitor in a Centro boutique hotel with restaurant meals and one valley excursion, and 250 dollars and up during Guelaguetza or Dia de Muertos, when lodging alone can consume the entire mid range figure.

On safety, Oaxaca state carries a US State Department Level 2 advisory, exercise increased caution, which is the same tier as much of the country and well below a do not travel designation. The practical risks in the city are petty theft in crowded markets and around festival crowds, and taxi overcharging, which is best managed by agreeing a fare before getting in since meters are uncommon. Carry cash for Hierve el Agua and rural markets, since there are no ATMs at the terraces. The commission driven mezcal tour is the most common commercial trap. Finally, be aware of the local context: tourism is up roughly 77 percent since 2020 and residents have protested over short term rentals, displacement and water access, so occasional demonstrations and road blockages are a genuine possibility rather than a curiosity.

rating_rationale.overall: The 1987 UNESCO listing pairing the colonial centre with Monte Alban, plus a food and mezcal culture with no real equivalent in Mexico, makes this a first-rank destination in almost any month.
rating_rationale.safety: Oaxaca state sits at a US State Department Level 2 advisory, exercise increased caution, with everyday risk in the city limited mainly to petty theft, taxi overcharging and occasional protest-related road blockages.
rating_rationale.value: Site fees are low at 210 pesos for Monte Alban and 105 pesos for Mitla and the airport colectivo is 135 pesos, but Day of the Dead lodging reportedly runs 50 to 80 percent above baseline with multi-night minimums.
